Extract and structure the following information as valid JSON only (no markdown, no extra text).Return ONLY this JSON structure:{"summary": "2-3 sentence summary of the video content","keyPoints": ["point 1", "point 2", ...],"aiConcepts": ["AI concept 1", "AI concept 2", ...],"technicalDetails": ["tool/framework 1", "implementation detail", ...],"visualContextFlags": [{"timestamp": 120, "reason": "Code demo", "context": "Shows Python implementation"}]}If the video is not about AI/tech, set aiConcepts and technicalDetails to empty arrays but still extract keyPoints.TRANSCRIPT:so AI agents are blowing up lately especially when you mix them with blockchains I recently tweeted about this AI agent called Fraser and it went super viral it got retweeted by Elon Musk and Naval and a couple of other massive names and in the comments I saw a couple of people asking how this works how do you make something like this and I was interested myself so over the weekend I decided to hack something together make my own AI agent that can perform onchain actions like submitting transactions sending funds between addresses interacting with smart contracts and even deploying smart contracts like a token so in this video I'm going to show you how to create this using two libraries one is just the open AI library and the second one is the VM library to actually submit these blockchain transactions and we're going to build this all in typescript so if you're excited let's get started the first thing I want to run through is how this actually works from like a conceptual level now the way that open AI implements this and it's going to be similar across different llms like Claude for example is this kind of assistant framework now there's a couple of entities I guess or like Concepts and how they relate to one another is like the fundamentals of how we're going to build this thing out so we're pretty much going to take this diagram and then implement it in code and that's going to be the full project right it's going to be capable of Performing unchain actions based on the messages and the interpretation of those messages so the first concept that we're going to talk about is this assistant so an assistant I can actually show you an example of this here is for example this uh open AI playground that I have here and an assistant you set the prompt let's say hello act like Shrek for example and I say hello Shrek this is the assistant right and you can pretty much configure the way that it is going to reply but not only that you can actually give it some functions on the right hand side here so it can act like a typical chat bot like chat GPT or Claude for example but you can also give it functions that give it this kind of superpowers to actually take the messages that it's receiving from the user interpret them and determine if it needs to run those functions so for example looking at the documentation here you can see that an example of a function would be get weather so if the user asks what's the weather like in Paris today the chatbot or the assistant as it's called is smart enough to say well the user is inquiring about the weather and I have a function called get weather I'm going to go ahead and run that function and these functions are kind of the game changer of what gives the assistance the superpowers to do stuff rather than just being a chatbot that can reply based on the knowledge that it's been trained with okay so if the user inquires about a specific thing for example you can imagine in this video we're going to say maybe something like deploy a token or send some funds to another address you can Implement whatever you want in code and I'll show you how to do that and then it's going to go ahead and execute the code that you've created for that tool or this function here okay and we'll talk about what tools and functions and assistants and their relation between them in the diagram in a moment here but this is the core concept here we're building an assistant that has tools and those tools can be whatever you want them to be so in this video we're going to create onchain actions and those are going to be set as the tools that the assistant can have at its disposal to call whenever it's determined that it should call those functions all right so that is the assistant that's where the personality comes from it's kind of the starting point of all of this process now we've created the assistant we're going to go ahead and create these threads here so threads you'll already be familiar with if you've ever used these tools before like chat gbt if I go to chat gbt and type in a new message on the home screen that's creating a new thread and the thread consists of back and forth messages between the user and the assistant so for example when I go to chat gbt or Claude what I'm doing when I create a new message for the first time here is hello Claude boom send that message I've created a thread with a message and you can see this back and forth behavior of message from the user message from the assistant so here you can see that's what this is describing here first my message was Hello this users message and then the assistant replies with whatever it deems necessary now what Claude and I just showcase doesn't demonstrate here is these functions so we just talked about the superpowers that you can kind of attach to the assistant and what's super interesting about this is each message that the user sends if I just said hello for example there's no real action there that that it needs to perform but if I asked it to say send an email or I don't know send a transaction on the blockchain or deploy a smart contract this user's message is going to be interpreted and if it deems that an action or a function is necessary to be executed it moves over to this run section here now a run is basically what happens when an action is determined to be required by the assistant so the assistant says oh I am a personal finance bot as you can see here the user asked something that I might need to execute an action for and it determines that by interpreting the user's message if it deems that an action or a function is necessary it kicks off a run so a run is basically the environment where the uh action or the function that you want to execute executes okay so let's say we're submitting a transaction we kick off a run and within that run we perform some actions like submit the transaction and return the hash for example of that transaction inside the Run we wait for it to execute and there's a couple different options here you can wait for it to execute by just polling it constantly or you can stream it back to the application that you're building in this simple demo we're just going to poll so the run executes the function submits a transaction for example or in this case does some calcul of retirement planning right it uses the function here you can see it's using Code interpreter there's a couple of different uh options for functions right now they have three different options one is like a code interpreter one is something else I can't remember the top of my head but the one we're interested in is these custom functions so you can write anything to execute here you can write uh return hello world for example you can write uh a full custom function that submits a transaction to the blockchain for example once that is done you can return something like transaction hash or return hello world and it takes that into the message that it's going to send back so basically the simplified flow here is interpret the message number one send it off to a run if it deems that an action is required and then run that thing and then number three here is actually create the message based on the action that was performed and send that back to the user so for example if we submitted the transaction we would provide the transaction hash back to the user from the action that we performed from the assistant message so as a stupid example let's generate like a function ask donkey how his waffles are going as an Ask donkey waffles function here on our Shrek system uh assistant so let's say I say hello Shrek the flow here is it's going to go into that diagram it's going to say whoops scroll down a bit here the user's message there's no action required here the interpretation is uh I don't have an action or a function to perform when the user just says hello so it doesn't go to this run it just simply sends an assistant message back so you can see here what brings you to the swamp today I want to know how donkey's waffles are going and if I send this it's going to interpret it and say hang on a minute I have this ask donkey waffles function let me go and call that and see what the result is so if I send this message you can see it's actually calling ask donkey waffles so here is where we would write the custom code to I don't know ping the donkey API or something like that you can see I haven't actually performed any uh functionality here so it's just asking us what this would return in theory let's say donkey says they are good from our API response or our function call response donkey says they're good this is the flow that we've just described in this diagram here so it goes into a run executes the function that you want to perform interprets the response from that function and sends it back so for example we created a message uh that says donkey's waffles are going well and in their response it says Hey donkey's waffles are good probably smells delicious too and then that's the message that gets back from that uh kind of side flow where it goes through the run and just to be super verbose about this if we say you are a blockchain assistant as the system message and then we generate kind of a dummy tool to say submit transaction to the blockchain and return the hash as the function here you can see we might need to to provide the chatbot with some data like uh details about the transaction in order for it to uh understand how to perform this function here let's click save and we can say hello this won't go through the Run flow but if we say uh Hey submit a transaction for me if it doesn't understand how to do it based on the inputs that it requires for example transaction data sender address recipient address here then it's going to inquire say hey I need this following information the amount of currency you want to send let's say 0.01 type of currency let's say eth send his address Ox let's just do the zero address and the recipients addressed the zero address hopefully it understands this and we can send that through so you can see now it's calling the submit transaction function that we defined with the user input provided so I said send it from 0 to 0 and 0.01 e then we can pretend the transaction goes through the transaction hash is 0x123 and that should provide that back to the user there so this is essentially what we're going to build we're just actually going to implement the functions where it can actually do this on the blockchain so now we're going to actually jump into some code in this video is going to be the first of many hopefully where I give you the fundamentals and create a kind of happy flow of getting started using onchain AI agents so we're just going to build a simple interface in the terminal we're just going to build the backend functions and basic functionality so you can take this and apply it to whatever you're creating I think this is a good starting point especially if you're getting started for you to create and understand exactly how this works and then go ahead and apply it to whatever you want to create so what we're going to do is we're first going to set up a simple typescript project we're going to get started with the openai SDK and then we're going to create a simple assistant with some placeholder functions and then we'll go ahead and install VM to actually apply the functions instead of just having them placeholders and write some functionality to perform some onchain actions within those functions and then we'll wrap it up with a demo to showcase the bot inside the terminal us having a conversation with it and it performing some unchain transactions so let's dive right into the code now this video does assume you know what you're doing when it comes to coding I'm not going to teach you the basics of uh how to write programs or how to set up your environment you need to have npm and no. JS installed we're going to use typescript in this tutorial so I'm going to assume you know the fundamentals I'm just going to teach you the basics of creating this agent so let's jump into the terminal Now Now setting up a typescript project from scratch in 2024 is somehow still a giant pain in the ass I don't know if I'm missing a tool or whatever but I'm just going to show you my opinionative the approach to doing things please feel free to customize it however you need so I'm just going to do npm init y we'll do npm install typescript at typ node and we'll do that as Dev dependencies we're then going to need to install open Ai and we're going to need VM as well so we'll do npm install open AI VM and these are going to be Dev dependencies too and in this video I'm going to use the EMV environment variable setup to provide a private key for the wallet of the chatbot that we're creating or the assistant that we're creating and the private uh sorry the environment variables for the open AI key that we're going to get so that we can actually pay for the chat messages that we're creating so we'll also do npm install. EnV one final optional thing I'm going to install is since we're doing this in a terminal uh interface in this video I'm going to do read line so I'll do mpm installed read line as well all right so that's going to be it for the dependencies we can open this up I'm going to be using uh cursor in this video uh I love cursor if you haven't tried it out would definitely recommend it okay so the fun begins we can create tsconfig do Json if you're using typescript I'm going to be using in this video and then this is a a config that I find works for me I don't know if it's the best I don't know if it's optimal whatever this works for me and we're just building a simp uh typescript back end here so you can feel free to copy this if you choose to if you actually know what you're doing when it comes to typescript configuration please feel free to customize this as you need now this next step is crucial okay if you're going to use your private key like I am one caveat to that is I'm using a private key that has zero real money okay this wallet that I'm going to use is only test net I would encourage you to create a new wallet if you do plan on using real funds there's a high percentage chance the AI is unpredictable okay please only play around with real money if you're comfortable for the AI to make a mistake and spend all of that real money okay this AI I don't control I don't know what's going to happen I'm just teaching you the fundamentals of how to create this system okay so what we're going to do is we're going to do a new file called G ignore and the first thing we're going to do is add the EMV file into this git ignore so this prevents you from uploading your environment variables which will contain your wallet's private key and your open AI API key to things like GitHub so that is Step number one we're also going to add dist in here and we're going to add node modules in here as well next thing we're going to do is jump into package.json we'll remove the tests I'm not going to be writing tests in this video and we're going to run a build command which runs TSC that will compile our typescript into JavaScript files and then we're going to do start which is going to be first we'll run build so we'll just do TSC and then we'll do and actually run the file that gets compiled so you can see in the configuration we're compiling compiling sorry to disc so we're going to go ahead and run node d/ index.js next thing we're going to do is create a source folder where all of our code is going to live so within here we're going to do an entry point called index.ts and we're going to write a main function called uh Main and within here we're going to do console.log hello world and we'll execute the main function this is just a little demo to actually make sure your typescript setup is running correctly so open up up the terminal within the IDE here you can see I'm using uh node.js version 20 if you have 18 I believe it'll be fine I'm just showing you my configuration if something doesn't work for you so I have 20.10.19 s and this is going to run this function and you can see the output here so just make sure your setup and environment is working correctly first what I'm about to do next is very important that you listen carefully if you're a new developer and you don't really understand what we're doing I'm going to create an environment variables file if you don't know what that is please look it up the environment variables file contains our sensitive values that's why we added it to the git ignore so we don't publish it when we put it on sites like GitHub in this file I'm going to add two variables one is going to be open AI API key you need to keep this a value secret the second value we're going to add is your wallet's private key do not use your main wallet do not use a wallet with real funds unless you are comfortable with losing all of your funds because the AI is going to control your wallet it is unpredictable it is an AI you don't control it I don't control it we don't really know what uh is happening under the hood it's essentially a blackbox so please first start out with a test wallet I recommend creating a new wallet wallet that has no real funds and is not associated to your main wallet in any way please be careful add it to the G ignore file don't publish it anywhere to GitHub make sure you don't share these values we're going to keep it a secret and that is very important all right so with that said we're going to create a EnV file here I'm going to put two values in first one is openai API key I will show you where to get that and the second one is the wallets private key that you want to use for this uh agent all right so I'll show you how to get an Opa op AI API key and I'll quickly show you how to get your wallet's private key as well so for me I use a coinbase wallet at least for my testing wallet it doesn't really matter the same thing will be available if you use metamask or you can generate one really doesn't matter um you just need to have some testing funds for the network that you're going to use all right so I jump into developer click show Private key type my password in here and I think it's going to be blurred so I can just show it anyway whatever and that is where you get your wallet's private key so go ahead and paste that value in one thing I will note is it needs to start with 0x so if you paste it in and it doesn't start with 0x just add 0x like this 0x to the front of it and paste it in if it already has that you don't need to add it just a little side note as for some reason it exports from some wallets and not others so paste it in if it starts with 0x that's all good you don't need to add this at the start but if it doesn't just add that in and paste this value here so I will click paste I'm not going to show you my private key because we don't leak our secret values next thing we going to do is go to platform. open.com and jump into the settings you'll need to create an account if you don't have one already go to the API Keys tab click new secret key and create a new secret key it will begin with something like sk- pr- whatever that is your secret key and you need to copy that value you can see I have a bunch of them here SK NASA that's pretty cool SK whatever you need that full value and you need to copy it and paste it into your vs code here under open AI API key so this will look like SKR Dash this will look like OS blah blah blah blah blah blah numbers and letters all right paste that in I'm going to paste them off screen because I don't want to show you those values so now we have our open AI API key we can make requests to the openai API to get responses back and we have a wallet for the uh agent that we just created so it has all the tools required to generate responses and perform actions on the blockchain so if you remember our diagram from the beginning we have a couple of things to do here number one is we need to create an assistant so we're going to have a function to create an assistant number two we need a function to create a thread the user is then going to send a message whoops this one into that thread so then we need to say well we're going to determine inside of a run so we're going to have a third function to create the run that run will go through a life cycle you can see that life cycle here it goes from in progress to hopefully created most of the time it can also fail it can be cancelled by you if you make a a request to cancel it or after like 10 minutes if the Run hasn't been handled it will expire so we need a fourth function to actually run the Run perform the actions required for the Run basically what I'm seeing here is the Run gets created it will go from an in progress state actually before that it's in a cued state q how do you spell cued I think it's like this cued and then it will go into in progress if the Run requires action it will go into a state called requires action step four is essentially handling this flow from in progress to requires action if it requires action that is step five so step five is going to be if it requires action that means it's identified specific functions that need to be called and we're going to call those functions and return that result in this message as a message from the assistant so it's pretty much the flow that I've just showed you just in a bit more detail and uh in more nuances I guess to how it actually works under the hood essentially though step one assistant step two is create a thread starts sending messages from the user into that thread here those messages will get interpreted inside of a run so we need to create a run in step three step number four is handle this life cycle so we'll manage is it in progress is it uh does it require action and like I mentioned earlier there's two different ways to approach this one is just to pull constantly retrieve the status of the run or you can actually stream it I haven't tried the streaming myself we're just going to pull it in this I think it's a lot easier and a lot cleaner to handle just as a basic getting started so step four is number step four is uh manage the life cycle of the run down here and that's step five is actually execute the identified functions based on the message that's been interpreted so essentially it will go from in progress requir action in this point we've identified between four and five what actions need to be called in number five we will actually execute those actions and finally the result of those actions is presented to the user in the form of an assistant message in the thread we created in step two so hopefully that's clear I know the 3 four and five kind of get up a little bit messy but if you're confused at the moment don't worry all we're doing is creating the steps that I showed you in the playground earlier in the video awesome so now within Source we're going to create a new folder called open Ai and we're going to create those five functions number one was create assistant. TS number two was create thread. TS number three was create run. TS so the user will send a message into the thread that will trigger a run now that the Run has been created it goes into that cued State and it will go through the life cycle so we need to inquire about the status of the run so that we can manage what to do when uh that run requires action or doesn't require action so to do that we're going to write a function called perform the Run basically this is just saying manage the runs life cycle if the runs life cycle moves into a requires action State meaning it needs to function call we therefore handle the function call so that is our fifth function in here which is going to be handle run tool call I call them tools I think tools and functions are kind of interchangeable basically the same thing so number one create Assistant two create thread three create run four perform the run and five if the Run requires actions handle those runs actions all right so the very first thing we're going to do is before we even go into those five steps we need to create an openai client so from the main function on that on line number one here we're going to import open AI from open Ai and essentially the first thing we want to do inside the main function here is const client is equal to new open Ai and we'll close it off there it automatically uh kind of reads in the environment variables file you don't need to add it in the configuration here within the create assistant function obviously we're going to do step number one which was create the open AI assistant so we're going to pass in the client here but first we need to import open AI again from open AI we're also going to import the assistant type from uh the resources beta depending on when you're watching this video I imagine it eventually moves out of beta so you might need to change that import b

editmore_vert{"summary": "This tutorial explains how to build an AI agent capable of performing on-chain blockchain actions, such as sending funds and deploying smart contracts. It utilizes the OpenAI Assistant API for conversational intelligence and the Viem library for executing transactions via TypeScript.","keyPoints": ["AI agents can be programmed to interact directly with blockchains using 'tools' or 'functions'.","The OpenAI Assistant framework relies on four main concepts: Assistants, Threads, Messages, and Runs.","Functions act as 'superpowers' that allow the AI to interpret a user's intent and execute custom code.","The development process involves setting up a TypeScript environment with specific libraries like Viem and dotenv.","Crucial safety measures include using testnets and protecting private keys to avoid loss of real funds."],"aiConcepts": ["AI Agents","OpenAI Assistant API","Function Calling / Tools","Threads and State Management","LLM Interpretation","Run Life Cycle (Polling vs. Streaming)"],"technicalDetails": ["TypeScript","Viem (Blockchain Library)","OpenAI SDK","Node.js","Environment Variables (.env)","npm/tsc configuration"],"visualContextFlags": [{"timestamp": 55, "reason": "Architecture Diagram", "context": "Visualizes the relationship between Assistants, Threads, Messages, and Runs."},{"timestamp": 125, "reason": "OpenAI Playground Demo", "context": "Demonstrates setting up a 'Shrek' assistant and configuring custom functions."},{"timestamp": 780, "reason": "Project Setup", "context": "Shows the terminal commands for initializing a TypeScript project and installing dependencies."}]}thumb_upthumb_down
